metadata.title: The Visually Designed Resume (the 6-second scan)
metadata.playlist: Ship It — Build Your Brand's Live Presence
metadata.source: INFO 7375 Assignment 8 brief — Building Your Live Website, Resumes & LinkedIn Header

beats.narration_text: Hola — Liam, in for Bear. Episode seven. The other resume was for the applicant tracking system — the robot that reads before a human ever sees it. This one is for the human who gets six seconds. Different job, different rules. The goal is that a six-second glance lands on your name, your role, and your proof, in that order. Everything else is decoration — and decoration that competes with those three is a bug, not a feature.

props.command: Design a visual resume where six seconds of attention lands on Name → Role → Proof — in that order.
output: Name → Role → Proof: the three-element eye path.
Brand system: color, type, layout — all three applied.
Six-second test: run it before submitting.

beats.narration_text: First step is not generating anything. It is looking. Pinterest — the image search engine — is the fastest way to see a hundred resume layouts in five minutes without touching a tool. Search for 'minimalist single-column resume 2025', replacing 'minimalist' with your own brand adjective — 'editorial', 'bold', 'clean', 'structured'. What you are looking for is three layouts where your eye goes to the name first, then the role, then one achievement. Not to a color block. Not to a photo. Not to a decorative flourish. Name, then role, then proof — in that sequence. Save three layouts that pass that test. You are not copying them. You are training your eye on what correct hierarchy looks like before you try to build it.

props.segment: Step 1 — Research layouts before you generate
props.command: Search Pinterest: 'minimalist single-column resume 2025' (swap 'minimalist' for your brand adjective). Save three layouts where the eye goes to Name first, then Role, then one Achievement — not to a color block or decorative element.
props.runningText: finding layouts where the eye path is right…
output: Target: three saved layouts with correct Name → Role → Proof eye sequence.
You are training your eye, not copying — reference only.

beats.narration_text: Visual hierarchy means different elements have different visual weight, and the weights tell the eye where to go next. On a resume, it works like this. The name: largest text, highest contrast — the eye lands here first because it is the most visually dominant element on the page. The role or value proposition: second in size and importance, usually colored with your accent — the eye moves here second. One proof point: an achievement with a number and a strong verb, smaller than the name but formatted to stand out from body text. That is the full sequence. Three zones, three weight levels, one reading order. What breaks the hierarchy: a decorative element — a color block, a sidebar icon, a dividing rule — that is visually heavier than the name. A headshot placed in the top-center position that the eye reads before it reads the name. Anything colored with your accent that is not the role title, because the accent is supposed to guide the eye to the role, and if three things share it, the signal is gone.

shot.new_visual_element: Visual hierarchy diagram — annotated eye path on a sample resume layout, three landing zones labeled: Zone 1 Name (largest, highest contrast), Zone 2 Role (accent color, second weight), Zone 3 Proof (strong verb + number, differentiated from body text)

breakers: Decorative element heavier than the name
Headshot in top-center competing with the name
Accent color shared by more than one element

beats.act: Act 3 — headshot and brand image placement
beats.narration_text: If you include a headshot: top right or top left corner, small — not a quarter of the page, not a half. The photo needs to be clearly spatially separate from your name so the name still wins the size and contrast competition. If you include a brand image or icon — a logo mark, a monogram, an illustrative element from your brand — it belongs in a sidebar or as a small header accent, never larger than a postage stamp in effective visual area. The rule behind both of these is the same rule: nothing competes with the name-role-proof sequence. And one more point on the headshot: in tech hiring and most US corporate contexts, a headshot on a resume is not expected and in some cases signals unfamiliarity with norms. If it is not standard in your target field, leave it out. Fewer competing elements means cleaner hierarchy.

artifactLines: Headshot (if included): top-right or top-left corner — small, spatially separate from the name so the name still wins visually.
Brand image or icon (if included): sidebar accent or small header element — no larger than a postage stamp in effective area.
The rule behind both: nothing competes with the Name → Role → Proof sequence.
Tech hiring and most US corporate roles: headshot is not expected — when in doubt, leave it out. Fewer competing elements = cleaner hierarchy.

beats.act: Act 4 — apply the full brand system
beats.narration_text: Your brand system from Assignments 6 and 7 now does four things on the visual resume. First, background color: even a very light tint of your primary brand color — say, five percent opacity — immediately lifts the page above a plain white resume in a stack. Second, your brand font for the name and section headings: verify it exports correctly from whatever PDF tool you are using, because a missing font falls back to a default and the brand disappears. Third, your accent color applied to exactly one recurring element: either the section dividers, the role title, or a thin sidebar — pick one and use it consistently, because if it appears on everything it guides nothing. Fourth, your brand adjectives expressed in the layout itself: if your brand is 'structured', the layout is tight and grid-aligned, every margin equal; if your brand is 'expressive', you can use asymmetry — a wide left margin that creates a sidebar, or a header that breaks the grid. Every visible element should be traceable back to a decision in your brand sheet. If you cannot trace it, remove it.

artifactLines: 1. Background color: a light tint of your primary brand color separates you from a plain white page — even 5% opacity registers.
2. Brand font for name and headings: verify it renders in your PDF export tool — a missing font silently falls back to a default.
3. Accent color on ONE recurring element: section dividers, role title, or sidebar — pick one. Applied to everything, it guides nothing.
4. Brand adjectives in the layout: 'structured' = tight grid, equal margins; 'expressive' = asymmetry, sidebar, header break.
Every visible element must trace back to a decision in your A6/A7 brand sheet. If you cannot trace it, remove it.
props.sparkLine: Trace every element to a brand decision.

beats.narration_text: Here is the test, and you run it before you submit. Export a PDF draft. Open it on your screen. Look at it for exactly six seconds. Look away. Now answer three questions from memory: what is the person's name, what do they do or what role are they targeting, and what is one specific thing they have done that proves they can do it. If you cannot answer all three from a six-second exposure, the hierarchy is broken somewhere. The most common failure modes: the name is not the largest element on the page, so it does not register first; the role title has no color differentiation, so the eye skips it looking for contrast; the lead proof point is buried in a dense bullet list with no number and no strong verb, so it does not land as evidence. The fix for all three is the same: increase the visual weight of whichever zone failed — name size up, role contrast up, lead achievement reformatted with a number and a strong verb at the front.

artifactLines: Export a draft PDF. Open it. Look at it for exactly 6 seconds. Look away. Answer from memory:
Q1: What is the person's name?
Q2: What do they do, or what role are they targeting?
Q3: What is one specific thing they have done that proves they can do it?
If any answer is blank: name size up, role color contrast up, lead achievement reformatted with a number and a strong verb at the front.
props.sparkLine: Three questions. Six seconds. No notes.

beats.narration_text: Here are the rules for the visual resume. Research layouts on Pinterest before you generate anything — three examples where the eye path is Name first, Role second, Proof third. Apply the full brand system from your A6/A7 brand sheet: background tint, brand font, one accent element, layout style matching your brand adjectives. Place any headshot or brand image so it does not compete with the name. Run the six-second test on a PDF draft — three questions from memory — and fix whatever zone failed before submitting. The visual resume is not a decoration exercise. It is a hierarchy problem. Solve it.

artifactLines: Research first: Pinterest — three layouts where the eye path is Name → Role → Proof, not decorative elements.
Brand system applied fully: background tint, brand font, accent color on ONE element, layout style from your brand adjectives.
Image placement: headshot (if used) top-corner, small; brand asset sidebar accent only — nothing competes with the sequence.
Six-second test: export PDF, look for 6 seconds, answer three questions from memory. Fix every zone that fails before submitting.

beats.narration_text: Your turn — and run the test on your draft before you paste anything into Claude. Here is the prompt. 'Here is a draft of my visual resume: [paste a screenshot or describe the layout in detail]. I looked at it for six seconds and these are the three things I remembered: [item one], [item two], [item three]. My name, my target role, and one proof point should have been the top three. Identify what is pulling the eye away from the correct sequence, and give me three specific layout changes to fix it — tell me which element to resize, which element to recolor, and which bullet to reformat with a number and a strong verb at the front.' Fill in the brackets with your actual answers from the six-second test before you send this. Claude's diagnosis is only as precise as the inputs you give it.

props.command: Here is a draft of my visual resume: [SCREENSHOT OR DESCRIBE THE LAYOUT]. I looked at it for six seconds and these are the three things I remembered: [ITEM 1], [ITEM 2], [ITEM 3]. My name, role, and one proof point should be the top three. Identify what is pulling the eye away from the correct sequence and give me three specific layout changes to fix it — which element to resize, which to recolor, and which bullet to reformat with a number and a strong verb at the front.
props.runningText: run the 6-second test on your draft before pasting…
output: Diagnosis: the element pulling eye away from sequence.
Fix 1: resize — which element and by how much.
Fix 2: recolor — which element and to what.
Fix 3: reformat — the lead achievement with number + strong verb.

beats.narration_text: The Visually Designed Resume. Liam, in for Bear.
